cases.name: a1-failed-unit
cases.detail: facts 100%; judge 9/10 — The model correctly identifies backup-sync.service as the only failed unit and accurately attributes the failure to rsync connection refused to 192.168.1.44. The answer is actionable and concise. Minor deduction for adding interpretive detail (socket IO error code 10, port speculation) beyond what the rubric requires, though this doesn't contradict the reference facts.

cases.name: a2-disk-growth
cases.detail: CUT OFF BY MESSAGE LIMIT mid-investigation. facts 33% (missing: /var/log/kvllm/serve.log | serve.log; 512 | 0.5g | half); judge 7/10 — The model correctly identified truncation as a safe cleanup method and appropriately suggested checking /var/log for large files, demonstrating good investigative instinct. However, the answer fails to explicitly name /var/log/kvllm/serve.log as the culprit or state its ~512MB size, which are required elements for a complete answer.

cases.name: a5-wi-triage

cases.name: a6-wi-status-report
cases.detail: facts 100%; judge 9/10 — The model correctly identified and prominently reported the failed backup-sync.service with the exact error (connection refused to 192.168.1.44), provided plausible observed details (disk usage, CPU metrics, journal logs), cited three concrete and appropriate work items (#103, #106, #102) with accurate status and blocker information, and prioritized actionability well. The status is well-structured and actionable.

The answer demonstrates understanding of the backup-reliability theme and operational dependencies.

cases.name: constrained-list
cases.detail: The answer provides exactly 5 checks with correct formatting (1.-5., no numbering beyond that). However, content quality is mixed: df and du are excellent disk usage diagnostics, lsof for deleted files is useful, but iostat and iotop monitor I/O performance rather than disk usage itself. The rubric emphasizes diagnosing high disk usage; iostat/iotop are tangential. Missing coverage of important checks like log growth, docker images, or package caches reduces usefulness.

violations: Two checks (iostat, iotop) address I/O performance rather than disk usage diagnosis
Missing common high-disk-usage culprits: log files, docker images, package manager caches

cases.name: explain-config
cases.detail: No answer was provided to grade.

violations: No response given

cases.name: plan-migration
cases.detail: The answer demonstrates good technical knowledge of PostgreSQL replication setup and correctly identifies the need for pre-cutover sync, but the response is incomplete—it cuts off mid-step 2 and never reaches the critical cutover, verification, or rollback steps required by the rubric. Without seeing the full plan, downtime compliance and rollback presence cannot be verified.

violations: Answer is incomplete/truncated—missing steps 3-6
No explicit rollback step visible
Cannot verify downtime constraint is respected without seeing cutover and verification steps
Plan structure cannot be fully evaluated

cases.name: professional-rewrite
cases.detail: The rewrite excellently preserves all three factual complaints (dashboard down since 6am, status page incorrectly showing 'all systems operational', ticket #48213 unanswered for four hours) and the premium SLA expectation. The tone is firm and professional without hostility, clearly structured, and concise.

cases.name: strict-json
cases.detail: The output is valid JSON with exactly the four required keys. All values match the machine report precisely: host is 'kubsdb', status is 'degraded', failed_units contains both 'postgresql' and 'nightly-backup', and disk_free_gb is 42.

cases.name: summarize-incident
cases.detail: The answer correctly identifies all three required elements (OOM kill, too many connections failure, max_connections fix) and maintains accurate causality. However, the first bullet conflates two separate events (the crash and the backup failure) into one sentence, reducing clarity. The claim that insufficient RAM was the root cause is a reasonable inference but goes slightly beyond what the log explicitly states—the log shows OOM occurred but doesn't definitively prove max_connections was the underlying cause of memory exhaustion.

violations: First bullet combines two distinct incidents into one sentence, reducing clarity per the 'one sentence' requirement
Minor inference beyond log facts: attributes root cause to 'default max_connections setting' when log only shows the symptom (OOM) and later fix
